kommunikationsstil.beschreibung: Person A writes like someone who can't sit with silence. Long messages follow non-responses, careful hedges sit next to sweet notes, question marks at the end of statements that aren't questions. That isn't clumsy — it's a precise tool to keep closeness from slipping.

horney.interpretation: When Person B goes quiet, Person A writes more. When there's tension, Person A is the first to take the step back toward warmth. That's not naivety — Person A has learned that pulling back grows the gap, so Person A invests toward the direction the fear comes from.
evidenz: Several messages after a long silence from Person B
"Sorry that was a lot" right after an emotional monologue
Sweet notes as de-escalation after every tension

berne.interpretation: Person A often speaks from a position of "hopefully this is okay for you". Hedges, softeners, a small apology at the end. But right alongside it — sometimes in the same sentence — something breaks through that is clear and direct: the question "What do you actually want from me?" is not a child-mode voice. That's the adult taking the wheel briefly and letting go again immediately.
evidenz: "Maybe we should talk?" packaged as a question, even though it's a request
"I think I'm too much" — adaptive child naming itself

bowlby.interpretation: Silence turns into danger for Person A. The response isn't withdrawal or anger, it's intensified contact. The longer Person B stays silent, the longer Person A's messages get — textbook anxious-ambivalent: protest without break, search without aggression. That a sweet note follows every confrontation isn't forgiveness — it's the fear that having-to-forgive could cost the relationship.
evidenz: Response intensity grows in proportion to Person B's reply time
After "I can't keep doing this" there's no consequence, just waiting
De-escalation always from the same side

adler.kompensation: Giving as insurance against being left
adler.interpretation: Person A gives early, a lot and visibly — messages, affection, attention. The unspoken hope: whoever gives enough won't be left. The catch: that very giving creates the asymmetry Person A is afraid of. Person A builds the dynamic Person A fears.
evidenz: The investment delta stays one-sided across all phases
Sweet notes after every perceived distance
Self-attribution "I'm too much" — not "You give too little"

goffman.front_stage: Warm, easy-going, inviting — Person A as someone who makes relationships feel light, demands nothing and offers everything.
goffman.back_stage_durchbrueche: Just before midnight the tone breaks. Long emotional messages, self-doubt ("I'm too much"), and then immediate playing-down ("Sorry that was a lot"). The backstage isn't invisible — it shows briefly and gets tucked away fast. More intentional than it looks.
goffman.interpretation: The front-stage performance "I'm low-maintenance" is the bill Person A writes themselves: if I'm easy, you'll stay. The back-stage moments are the ones where that math stops adding up — and Person A clears them away quickly, before anyone sees too much.
evidenz: 11pm-ish messages: full emotion, immediately followed by apology
"I'm just emotional right now" as a meta-cover

sprachliche_fingerabdruecke.zeichensetzung: Three dots instead of a period. Question marks on statements that aren't questions ("I think we should talk?"). Emojis as a soft landing after hard lines — especially 💕 and 😊, almost never 😅 or 🙄. Not a single word in all caps.

kern_insight: Person A gives earlier, more and more visibly than needed — and calls it love. Which it also is. And at the same time, an insurance policy against the sentence that must never be said.
